Hey, this is Russell again. Welcome back and today I'm excited to introduce you to somebody that, his name is Ted Thomas and he taught me this concept of trial closing. And this whole presentation is going to be about trial closes. So what is a trial close? How does it work? Why is it so important? As you'll see in the interview, Ted and I will get deeper into this, but it's a concept where my trial closes are things I'm saying to my presentation to get you simply to nod your head yes, to say the word yes. I remember I had heard rumors of Ted Thomas, they used to call him the Pied Piper of closing because when he would get done closing like a Robert Kiyosaki event or things like that, there'd be a line of people following him to the back of the table with their credit cards in hand. And he became so famous because of that and I remember I had a chance where he actually showed up one of my events and we'll go deeper in this in the interview, but after I spoke he pulled me aside, took me to lunch and he said, if you notice something about the way you sell versus the way I sell. He said, all the people who were listening to you were engaged. He's like, I sat in the back of them, they're all engaged, but none of their heads were moving. He's like, I want somebody to say yes to me not like once or twice before asking for money. I want them to say yes a hundred times or more before I ever ask them for money. He said, they've been saying yes, yes, yes to me over and over and over again. By the time I ask for money, it's not that difficult for them to say, yeah, I'm going to give you money, right? He said, if you look in the back of them and I'm speaking, if you look at their heads, it's like a sea of people's heads nodding. Just yes, yes, yes, the entire presentation. He said, that's what you're missing in your presentations. He gave me that hint and then he kind of explained what trial closes were and I didn't know at the time and they were just very simple questions to get somebody to say yes. Does that make sense? I just use the trial clothes. Does that make sense? You probably said yes and your head nodded subconsciously, right? I'm just asking a little question like, does that make sense? Are you guys getting this? Do you think this would work for you inside of your business? I'm asking you really basic questions. The only logical answer is yes. People are saying yes in their heads. What will happen in your audience, you'll start seeing people's heads are nodding and you'll see them smiling and like you see the entire audience will move their heads. If you're at home on a webinar, sometimes I'll have fun with this. I say, look, I know you're sitting behind your computer and you're doing whatever you're doing, but if you're excited, I'll shake your head, yes. I want to make sure you guys are actively participating, nod your head, yes, right now. I'll get people to actually do that even though they're not sitting in front of me, but I want them to say yes as many times as possible before I ever ask them for money. As you're doing this and you start kind of time-lining out your presentation, right? If you've been following the perfect webinar script that I've kind of given you guys throughout this training, there's a structure, right? There's like, here's my one thing, here's my three secrets, here's my transition, here's my stack and my close. We have kind of the framework of how that's working. What your job is to do is then start weaving in these trial closes so that throughout your presentation you are doing things and saying things and asking these little questions to get somebody to say yes 20, 30, 50, 100 times before you ever ask them for money. Because they said yes all that many times. By the time you're like, hey, can you give me some money for this amazing offer? They're like in this state of like yes, yes, yes, and they're more likely to say yes at that point. And so that's kind of what trial closes are and you're going to love them. And you'll see in the interview, Ted and I get into this. It's not just getting them to say yes throughout the presentation. There's certain times it's like even more powerful. For example, if I share a success story or a case study, after I do that, I don't just move on to the next thing. I stop and I drop a bunch of trial closes. If you watch my presentation at the 10X event, I shared Liz Benny's testimonial. After I shared it, I stopped. I said, okay, now can you imagine what it'd be like to be Liz? She was just like you four months ago. She had never had success, but she followed this process and she made $100,000 for the very first month. Can you imagine what it'd be like? What would your spouse or your family think about that? That'd be pretty amazing. Don't you think? Like, oh, I don't know about you, but that would radically change my life. Would that change your life forever that happened to you? And I asked four or five questions and said yes. And then I share the one of Mike Stanzik. I share his story. Instead of just moving on and telling Mike's story, I'm like, man, how many of you guys have been trying to make money online for three months, six months, or longer? What would it be like to be successful in your first try? Can you imagine that? What would it be like to go tell your family or your friends that? Like, wouldn't that be cool? And I asked a couple of yes questions. They share Simon Crier's testimony and then Drew Canoies. And every time I share a testimony, I stop and I drop in a whole bunch of trial closes afterwards, four or five or six. I want them to visualize that. I want them to say yes, I want that. Yes, that could be me. Yes, I can see myself doing that. Yes, yes, yes. Then move to the next one. So by the time I've done just my testimonial section right there, I've got 30, 40 yeses from them. I'm going to come over here and say, hey, do you want to give me money to have that same thing happen to you? They're already shaking their head, right? It makes it so much easier. So trial closes are magic. You guys will learn a ton from this interview with Ted. And I hope you guys enjoy it. And I'll post down in this page down below as well the article that I kind of shared in the interview that shows exactly all the trial closes I used in my auto webinar. I'll talk about this, but there was basically an auto webinar presentation I had that was already pre-recorded. It was done. It was live. It was running. It was doing really really well. I went back to my, I literally went back to my timeline presentation in our video editing software and I found where I could put a trial close and I recorded it. I recorded all these trial closes and my brother just edited them into the video, re-rendered out, plugged it in. It went from like $9.60 per register to like $16.40, I believe. So I'll put it in the article down below so you can see like here's the actual trial closes Russell plugged into his automated webinar presentation. You can see those as an example, but this little skill set, like at first it seemed kind of weird and awkward, but eventually they become part of your language patterns. Like I say trial closes all the time. If you watch my Facebook lives, my webinars, my tele seminars, my stage, anytime you see me speak, you'll notice me doing that all the time, right? Cause it's happening, right? Right, right, right, right. Those are all trial closes. So I have fun to interview Ted Thomas. He's a lot of fun to hang out with. I hope you enjoy it. I hope you guys start plugging in these trial clothes into your presentation. Hey everyone, this is Russell Brunson. I'm here today with a legend and I'm so excited to be interviewing him. His name is Ted Thomas and I'm going to tell you guys kind of the story behind this and we're going to dive into kind of his zone of genius. And I mean, there's so many things to talk about. There's one specific thing I really want to talk about. And I had heard rumors about Ted as I was growing up in this industry from a lot of my mentors and they told me and I heard stories about him selling from stage and like lines of people following to the back of the room with their credit cards and hands. And I like heard these rumors. I was like, that is the coolest thing ever. And I like searched YouTube videos. I'd seen some footage of it. And then one day I was speaking at an event. I can't remember who's it. Oh, I think it was Dustin and Dave's event. I was speaking and do my thing. I look out in the audience and I see you and I had this like look of fear like, oh no, like he's, he's seeing me pitch and like, I was so nervous and I was so scared. Other than that, just plug trial closes in. Then we kept driving traffic to the new version and we went from making $9 and 45 cents per webinar registered to $16 and 50. Wow. Wow. Here's by adding his trial closes. And I was like, Ted is a genius and I've been doing trial closes. Station in my life ever since then. So, oh, that's great. That's good. Good for you. Yeah. But you know, you're a fast study. Most people aren't that quick, but you know, if you do that, what you did is you automatically did what's natural to you and you engage the audience. You see, by doing the trial clothes, you were just nice enough to ask them a question and let them response. And you know, a lot of speakers, they just blast the audience. You know, they just wham, they hit them. It's so hard. But if you just slow it down a little bit and ask them, well, would that work for you? Does this make sense? You know, a few simple questions and all of a sudden the people said, yeah, that makes sense to me. And, and, and, you know, you can, you can take it to another level and then, you know, ask them to answer out loud and throw tomatoes at you and stuff. You know, the more engaged they get, the more they'll spin. And it's amazing. And it's just natural. It's not a, it's not something that now it's not natural for you to talk that way because everybody thinks they have to sell everyone. You don't have to sell them. You just have to get them to agree with you. You have to get them to trust you and feel comfortable with you. And that's what you did by slowing it down. Does it make sense to you? And I was like, Oh my gosh, like it just, anyway, it was amazing. So let's go back in time. Okay. How did you get into this whole speaking world? Like what was your, I'm curious like how you, how you came into this, this world. Okay. All right. Well, let's take you way back. My first career was as an airline pilot. Okay. So that's a very restrictive job. You got to, it's a very good job. You know, people fly all over the world and do a lot of great things, but you've got to follow the rules. I mean, everything's followed the rules. And so when I discovered I could, that I could make money in business, I actually resigned from the airlines and I went into a business, a real estate, which was very, very good to me. It was very good. But when it crashed, oh my goodness, it came down on top of me. And all of a sudden I was like, I got it was wealthy and I was real broke. And so I said, I'm going to devise something that is, is going to be secure. So when I built that business, I didn't know how to publish and I did not talk to people. And so I said, I'm going to relearn. So I figured if I could learn how to fly airplanes, I could relearn how to get along with some people. So what I did is I created, I'm like you, I have info, info products. And so the, the info product I created people saw a comment, come and show it to me. So the first book I ever did, first book I ever did, I wrote the book and it was a big, thick monster like this, just crazy monster, 465 pages. Well, nobody will buy that. I didn't know that. But, you know, I want to tell you everything I knew, right? So 465 pages. And I took it to, to John Wiley and son, which is a big publisher in New York City, big publisher. And they said, are you kidding? They tore the book in half. They tore the book in half. They published it. It was in the bookstore for 12 years. First book was the best seller. First book. I said, oh my God, how did I do that? So I, but then people said, well, tell me about the book. Well, I would do like everybody does. I would tell them all the facts and the figures and everything. And everything just sounded so good. I mean, I knew those facts and figures like that, you know, but guess what? Nobody bought anything. And then I said, well, you know, if you bought this and you did this, you'd make an extra $10,000 a month. And then I would say, how do you like that? The person's over done. Right. I like to make $10,000 a month. And then as soon as they answered me, I started to get it. Now I got a pretty hard head. So I had to ask those questions a lot of times. And so then I started to realize, oh, those are trial closes. And so you can go in the marketplace and you can find four or five hundred books on sales, the average sales person's never read one, let alone any of the four or five hundred. So I started speaking on the platform and I've got to tell you, I know what it is to have a big audience and get all finished and you want them to buy something and you ask them to come and buy it and that everybody, when they leave, they walk out that door and that door. They won't walk right at you because they're so embarrassed. And you walk home, you say, gee, I didn't make very much money on that sale. Meanwhile, I might have flown all the way to Los Angeles or something. Right. So that was kind of crazy. So I learned how to do that step by step. And then I started integrating it into the talks as you did. So you're a quick, you're a quick study. You heard me say it. You knew what my reputation was. So you did it. So I already had trust and rapport with you. The hardest thing to build with an audience is trust and rapport. And the audience said, ah, this guy's going to pitch me. I don't want to be pitched. I don't want to buy nothing. You know, they'll still say that. But if you say, well, look, if I could show you how to make an extra twenty thousand dollars this month and you had to work three or four hours, would that be of interest to you? You see, I always end everything with a question. And the question is a gentle answer. I mean, I'm not going to take you. Keep your credit card in your pocket all day. I don't get taken home with you. Think about what I'm talking about. And so once they trust, then you can make the next step. But you can't do it. So that's how I got started. I just kept it going. And then I have wonderful days like work. I've been on the Tony Robbins program. You know, people that you've done to Tony would say, all right, go ahead up there and give me, do your stuff or go to Kiyosaki. Some of the big ones. Well, you know, when you walk to the back table and you've made 150 sales, the money doesn't matter. Your ego is a little bigger than the Empire State. A little bigger than that. You know, you're just, you know, you're 10 foot tall and you're bulletproof walking back there, right? All these people following you and then you take a movie of it and you show all your friends the movie. Oh, it's wonderful. So, so you got to do some stuff like that. Now, don't think that it's always perfect because sometimes you go back and you say, oh God, I wish somebody would buy something and they don't. You know, so then it's not the, it's never the audience's fault ever. It's your fault. You didn't talk to them. That's what's wrong. You don't talk to them. You talk, you talk to a bunch of 80 year old people about the internet and they're going to say, forget it. Just bring me the New York Times. I'll be happy with that. You know, they're not going to do it. But you get a bunch of 18 year olds that are sophomores in college. They want to know how to get rich next week. And so you say, well, this will work for you, right? And they say, yeah, yeah, let me, let me do it. They'll go try it even because they're not afraid. It's, it's, it's just you've developed a rapport. So that's what trial closes are is developing a rapport with that client. For someone who's, this is the first time ever hearing a trial closes, like, um, I guess what are, what are examples? Like just like some examples of how you use them, where you use them and, and just kind of the psychology behind it. Well, first you have to do what we're doing today. Understand that there is such a thing and understand that professionals will work towards helping you. So you're a professional. See, at your point in life and a lot of people at my age in life, money isn't the most important thing. What's really the most important thing is that you can genuinely help someone and you can do something that makes you, you feel good and then feel good. So the first thing you have to do, if you want to be a trial closer is understand that the client wants help. And so when you make a statement of any kind, when you make that statement, you understand what it went. So you need to ask him, well, you think that's going to be helpful for you. Uh, what would your wife think if you were able to make an extra $4,000 this week and you end things with us? Gentle question. You don't, you don't do like a, uh, police detective and ask them questions because that's an integration. You don't want to be, you don't want to be, you know, going after the client that way, but what you do want to do is you want to ask gentle questions all the time. Uh, when I do a, uh, presentation, even though I've been doing the presentation for decades, I will incorporate anywhere from 35 to 105 trial closes in one hour and 30 minutes. Wow. This happened all the time. It's not just like, there's my three trial closes today. It's like all the time. You want to integrate those all the time. And so people that, um, that haven't done it, they need to start observing it first of all, or they should start studying it. So don't, once you repeat it, uh, never underestimate the audience. The audience is smart. So use different trial closes throughout the process.
